Lead Applicant.Organisation: GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST
Lead Applicant.Department: Therapies Professional Background Allied Health Professional Detailed Professional Music therapist Background Professional Registration HCPC Arts Therapist: AS15858

APPLICATION DETAILS.Patient & Public Involvement:
Music therapy is collaborative by nature and, in paediatric care, must be shaped entirely by what matters
to children, young people and families. Music therapy research should be no different. Community
Engagement and Involvement (CEI) has been part of my research from the beginning: during my GOSH
Biomedical Research Centre (BRC) internship, I spoke with six families from diverse backgrounds
(Bangladesh, Ghana, Romania, Ireland, UK) about my research plan. Short conversations at convenient
times, with plain-English summaries provided, demonstrated respect for families’ time. Families felt the
research was worthwhile and expressed interest in being involved in my project. Four carers highlighted
that fixed-time meetings could be difficult due to the unpredictability of inpatient care; two noted the
challenge of balancing hospital attendance with childcare. Two families for whom English was not a first
language suggested receiving translated information and questions in advance. These early discussions
have already shaped how I will approach my research during both my predoc award and doctoral
studies. Their reflections highlighted the need for research processes that fit around the realities of
inpatient life: families’ emphasis on unpredictability, competing caregiving demands and emotional
fatigue reinforced my commitment to designing research that is flexible, responsive and paced by
participants’ capacity at any given time. Feedback from families for whom English was not a first
language reinforced the importance of prioritising clear, accessible communication, including translated
materials and advance sharing of questions, to enable meaningful engagement.
I will work with 8-10 parent or carer contributors and, where appropriate, 3-5 young people with
experience of inpatient music therapy. Recruitment will take place in collaboration with clinical teams,
using sensitive and timely approaches, either in person or via brief phone calls. Recognising the time
and emotional pressures on families in inpatient settings, their early involvement will be flexible and
informal conversations, exploring priorities and experiences, including what mattered during hospital
stays, challenges within music therapy, and meaningful outcomes. Emerging findings will be shared in
accessible ways, with invitations for feedback (“Here’s what I’m finding so far - does this reflect your
experience?”). This will help capture nuance and context that might otherwise be overlooked.
To ensure my research remains grounded in real-world priorities, CEI will be integrated across key
stages: project set-up (refining research questions); data analysis (sense-checking emerging themes,
validating findings, and identifying gaps or alternative perspectives); and dissemination (co-creating
accessible summaries and presentations; deciding how results are shared).
I will aim to ensure contributors reflect, as far as possible, the diversity of families accessing inpatient
services. I will work with GOSH PPIE leads to support inclusive involvement and reduce barriers to
participation, including the use of interpreters or translated materials where required, and adapting
methods for families with disabilities or additional needs. Contributors will be paid for their time.
By involving people with lived experience at every stage, I aim to produce research that is relevant,
respectful and genuinely informed by those it is intended to benefit.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP: Organisation King's College London Email Address kirsty.jane1@nhs.net Role Mentor Description of Support Kirsty Jane is a specialist music therapist with expertise in neonatal Provided palliative care. She is a Doctoral Clinical Academic Fellow at King’s College London and was awarded an NIHR-funded Doctoral Clinical Academic Fellowship in 2023. She has extensive experience in collaborative methods and PPIE, working closely with people with lived experience to increase the accessibility of psychotherapeutic support and dissemination of findings. Her work focuses on improving the acceptability and accessibility of music therapy for parents, carers, and healthcare professionals during uncertainty. Ms Jane will serve as an excellent sounding board for Katya on the clinical realities and therapeutic nuances of music therapy, ensuring the realist review remains clinically meaningful. She will also provide invaluable support for Katya’s professional development as a clinical academic, including guidance in preparing for future doctoral studies and effective patient and public engagement and involvement. Katya will meet with Ms Jane monthly to discuss the development of PPIE that will inform future doctoral fellowship applications, creative and accessible methods for dissemination of findings as well as reviewing the clinical relevance and implications of the findings for practice. Full Name Ms Jasmine Edwards Organisation New York University (USA) Email Address Jne254@nyu.edu Role Mentor Description of Support Jasmine Edwards is a practising Music Therapist and Doctoral Provided Candidate and Fellow at New York University, with a focus on cultural humility, inclusive practice, and the lived experiences of under- represented communities. She has served as adjunct faculty in Music Therapy departments at Howard University, NYU, Montclair State University, Nazareth College, Duquesne University, and Molly University. Ms Edwards will provide mentorship to Katya in developing culturally responsive research questions that are driven by the experiences and voices of the children and families served. She will support the sensitive interpretation of diverse patient and family perspectives, and the embedding of equity and inclusion throughout the review methodology, aligning the project with NHS priorities on inclusive practice. Katya will meet with Ms Edwards quarterly to ensure these principles are consistently applied throughout the project. Looking forwards, and in addition to project-specific mentorship, Ms Edwards will support Katya Herman’s development throughout her progression toward a clinical academic career. Drawing on her experience as both a practising clinician in paediatric hospitals, and doctoral fellow, Ms Edwards will provide guidance on balancing clinical practice with academic responsibilities, developing a sustainable research identity, and navigating the expectations of doctoral study. Through regular mentoring discussions, Ms Edwards will support Katya in building confidence as an independent researcher, strengthening interdisciplinary collaboration skills, and positioning her work to contribute meaningfully to both clinical practice and academic scholarship. Full Name Professor Jo Wray Organisation GREAT ORMOND STREET HOSPITAL FOR CHILDREN NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address jo.wray@gosh.nhs.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Jo Wray is an experienced health psychologist at Great Provided Ormond Street Children’s Hospital, London and Professor of Child Health Psychology – Cardiology, Critical Care and Transplantation in the Institute of Cardiovascular Science at UCL. She leads a programme of psychosocial research at GOSH with a particular focus on the psychosocial impact of chronic and rare health conditions in children and patient-reported experience and outcomes. She is a senior research fellow in the Centre for Outcomes and Experience Research in Children’s Health, Illness and Disability (ORCHID) at GOSH. Across her GOSH and UCL roles she supports nursing and allied health research at all levels. She has supervised students at all stages of the clinical academic pathway including nine PhD students (three of whom have been NIHR funded) and four clinical psychology doctoral trainees to successful completion, together with eight Master’s students. She is currently supervising nine doctoral students, five of whom will complete in 2026/7. As Professor Wray works in the same institution as the applicant, provision of regular supervision will be easily accommodated, and she has protected time to provide academic supervision. Professor Wray is a mixed methods researcher and brings methodological expertise to the current predoctoral application and planned doctoral application. She has considerable experience of working with children, families and staff using a wide range of methodologies, including focus groups, on-line forums, interviews and questionnaires (including questionnaire development) and has also undertaken a number of scoping and systematic reviews. She has published over 200 peer-reviewed articles and contributed to a number of international guidelines. Professor Wray is currently leading or a co- applicant on several national and international funded studies which include qualitative approaches to exploring patient, family and staff experiences of illness and transition to adult services, quantitative elements related to clinical and psychosocial data, systematic, scoping, and realist reviews and evidence synthesis. Professor Wray and Katya have previously collaborated: Professor Wray provided guidance to create and develop the Patient- and Family- Reported Experience Measures used by the Music Therapy service at GOSH. This established working relationship demonstrates Professor Wray’s knowledge of Katya’s capabilities and provides a strong foundation for continued mentorship. For this project, Professor Wray will provide academic leadership and methodological supervision, supporting Katya in realist review methodology, critical appraisal, qualitative synthesis, and academic writing. Professor Wray will meet monthly with Katya to review progress, discuss emerging findings, and provide structured mentorship, including guidance on publication strategy, and engagement with research networks. She will also ensure the review is contextualised within the psychosocial and clinical challenges faced by hospitalised children and families, strengthening the translational potential of the findings. Professor Wray will ensure Katya’s academic development needs are met during the NIHR Pre-Doctoral Fellowship and she will continue to support the development of Katya’s research ideas and advancement of her future academic career as she prepares her doctoral fellowship application. Full Name Professor Dr Annie Heiderscheit Organisation Anglia Ruskin University Email Address Annie.Heiderscheit@aru.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Annie Heiderscheit is Professor of Music Therapy and Provided Director of the Cambridge Institute for Music Therapy Research at Anglia Ruskin University. She is a board-certified music therapist (MT- BC) with over 32 years of advanced clinical practice and more than 25 years’ experience as a music therapy educator and researcher. Professor Heiderscheit has an outstanding international reputation for leading interdisciplinary research programmes and has methodological expertise in evidence synthesis, systematic reviews, and the evaluation of complex music-based interventions across paediatric, medical, critical care, transplantation, and mental health contexts. Her research is firmly grounded in clinical practice and focuses on improving patient and family outcomes during hospitalisation, including organ and bone marrow transplantation and other high-acuity care pathways. She has made a substantial and sustained contribution to the field, authoring over 50 peer-reviewed journal articles, more than 35 book chapters, and five authoritative textbooks on music therapy practice and research. This body of work demonstrates her leadership in advancing rigorous, clinically relevant evidence to inform healthcare practice and policy. Professor Heiderscheit has extensive experience supervising postgraduate research students, having successfully supervised three PhD students and approximately forty master’s students to completion. She is currently supervising two PhD students and eleven master’s students across early-, mid-, and late-stage study, demonstrating sustained supervisory capacity and experience supporting trainees at different stages of academic development. Professor Heiderscheit’s expertise aligns directly with the aims and methodology of Katya’s proposed fellowship, particularly in relation to paediatric music therapy within medical settings and the robust evaluation of psychosocial and experiential outcomes. She will provide senior methodological mentorship on the theoretical and practical foundations of a realist review, including protocol development, critical appraisal, synthesis of qualitative and quantitative evidence, and transparent reporting in line with best practice standards. This will ensure the review achieves a high level of disciplinary rigour and relevance to clinical care. In addition, Professor Heiderscheit will support Katya’s development as an emerging clinical academic by fostering specialist expertise in paediatric music therapy and guiding the translation of evidence into clinically meaningful and implementation-focused insights. She will meet with Katya monthly to provide structured supervision, including progress review, methodological troubleshooting, interpretation of findings, and strategic career development advice. Professor Heiderscheit brings a combination of extensive clinical experience internationally recognised methodological expertise, and proven leadership in music therapy research, placing her in an exceptional position to support Katya in successfully delivering the fellowship objectives. Her mentorship will play a critical role in equipping Katya with the skills, confidence, and academic foundation required to progress towards a sustainable career as a clinical academic aligned with NIHR priorities.
SUMMARY BUDGET: The requested funding will provide essential support for my development as a clinical academic, enabling me to undertake research training, attend relevant conferences, and engage in patient and public involvement activities. This will build the skills, knowledge, and networks required to prepare a competitive Doctoral Award application. Each cost has been carefully considered to ensure value for money, directly supports the proposed work, and aligns with the NIHR’s objectives of developing high- quality, patient-centred research. Salary Costs (£88,060.70) Funding for my salary will provide protected time to undertake comprehensive research training, develop academic skills, and engage meaningfully with patients and communities. Covering my salary through this award will allow me to fully commit to my Master’s in Research (MRes) training, acquire both practical and theoretical training on design and conduct of a research project, conduct my realist review, and prepare a competitive Doctoral Award application. This support is essential to build the foundation for a sustainable career as a clinical academic. Training UCL Child Health MRes Fees (£16,800) This component of the award is critical given my limited prior research experience. The Child Health MRes will provide advanced training in research methodology, statistics, and study design specifically tailored to paediatric health research. The course at UCL was chosen carefully for its relevance to my clinical background and its focus on training researchers to conduct meaningful, patient-centred studies. Modules also cover working with children and families, research ethics, and dissemination of findings. Completing the MRes will equip me with the skills necessary to design and deliver a robust research project and prepare a competitive Doctoral Award application. Cultural Competence Training at the Centre for Ethnic Health Research (£500) This training covers inclusive, accessible, and culturally sensitive approaches to CEI, ensuring that lived- experience perspectives are meaningfully incorporated into research design, interpretation, and dissemination. By enhancing my understanding of the cultural and social factors that influence engagement, it will improve the quality, relevance, and impact of the research while supporting ethical, patient-centred practice. Conference Attendance (£1,000) I plan to attend two conferences directly relevant to my proposed research in paediatric music therapy: the 12th Nordic Music Therapy Conference, and the British Association for Music Therapy Conference 2026. Attendance at these conferences will support dissemination of early work, provide feedback from experts in the field, and contribute to the development of a competitive future Doctoral Award application. The total requested amount of £1,000 is broken down as follows: Nordic Music Therapy Conference (£750 total) Registration: £350 Travel: £250 (conference is in Iceland) Accommodation (2-3 nights, budget rate): £150 British Association for Music Therapy Conference (£250 total) Registration: £250 These conferences will enable me to present preliminary ideas, engage with current methodological developments in music therapy research, and build professional networks with clinicians and researchers in my field. Funding will ensure full participation, strengthening my academic development and future research applications. Community Engagement and Involvement (£1,320) Funding is requested to compensate families for their time in four short, informal meetings to provide feedback and guidance on the research and dissemination. I anticipate approximately 6-8 families participating, with a possible 2 family members per family, giving around 12 participants in total. Each session will last approximately one hour with little or no preparation, in line with NIHR guidance (£27.50 per hour). This ensures fair reimbursement for family involvement while acknowledging the realities of inpatient family schedules. Reimbursement acknowledges participants’ time and expertise, supporting meaningful involvement in shaping research questions, interpreting findings, and co-producing dissemination outputs. This approach ensures the research is patient-centred, ethically robust, and relevant to families’ real-world priorities. Supervision and Mentoring (£1,000) This covers the time of two experienced supervisors and two mentors, providing project oversight, methodological guidance, and professional support. Supervision ensures high-quality research conduct, adherence to ethical standards, and guidance for my career development as a clinical academic. Overall the requested funding provides comprehensive support for my development as a clinical academic, ensuring protected time, relevant training, mentorship, and engagement with both the research community and families.
